paragraphs.text: THE LIFE OF THE SOUL THE DESIROUS ASPECT The desirous aspect ofthe life ofthe soul. Its function. The healthy and disorderly states ofthe appetitive capacity.

The Aspect ofDesire. The faculty that operates here is the will, which wills, desires to acquire, use or do what is beneficial to itself or which is necessary or pleasant; in addition, it does not will or desire anything which is contrary or unpleasant.

Disturbances of the will demand a corresponding act, because the will is in fact the active power, the essential need of which is to live and function. It maintains under its supervision all powers of the soul and body, and all functions which are at its disposal, all of which are put to use when needed. At its foundation lies zeal, or ardor—the thirst for something. Its stimuli are the pleasant, beneficial and necessary, when these are present, zeal sleeps, and the active powers lose their tension and fall away. They support desire, while desire kindles zeal.

The mental aspect may be analyzed as follows. In the soul and body are needs, including such ordinary ones as family and social needs. These needs in and of themselves do not constitute any distinct desire, but merely form the pretext for seeking gratification for themselves. When a need is satisfied in a particular way on a given occasion, then ever afterward, whenever this need is aroused, along with it is born the desire for that which has satisfied it previously. Desire always has a distinct object: a satisfied need. Another need has been satisfied in diverse ways; that is why when it is aroused, a diversity of desires is born—first this, then that, then a third thing capable of 53

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE satisfying that need. W^hen we analyze man s life, the needs behind the desires are not even apparent. Only the desires burrow into the soul and demand satisfaction, as if for their own sakes.

What does the soul do about these desires? It is presented with a choice as to which object it will give preference from among all those which has been set before it. Along with this choice comes a decision—to make, to attain or to use the chosen object. In accordance with the decision, means are selected, a method is defined, and a procedure for carrying it out. After all of this comes the act in its own good time and place. Everything, even the most trifling act, goes according to this procedure. You may check this yourself with your own routine. Sometimes all these operations are carried out instantaneously by habit, and the act follows immediately upon the desire. Choice, decision and means are then undertaken from previous acts, and do not demand a specially formulated basis.

In a person who has lived for some time, almost everything is done by habit. Rarely does any new enterprise or undertaking occur which departs from the usual order of business and knowledge. So it is that the life which has been established demands acts corresponding to itself. As these acts repeat themselves often, it is natural that they become habits, ways and rules of life and character. The way of life of a particular person is established from the sum total of all habits, rules and procedures of this type, in such a manner that the way of his thinking and his outlook are made up of this sum total of preestablished notions. Knowing someone’s way of life, it is possible to guess what he is thinking at any given time, and how he will act in given situations.
